speaker: interviewer
text: February 28th, 1995-- Interview with Rabbi Jacob Jungreis. Interviewer, Ricky Halperin, Brooklyn, New York. Interview conducted in English. Could you please state your full name, Hebrew name, any nicknames, and your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, my Hebrew name is Jacob Jungreis, and in Europe it was a good omen, also, to have a Gentile name, so in Hungarian and to this day on my passport, I'm called Nicholas Jungreis. And I was born February 4, 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city and what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Derecske, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me a little bit about the city that you were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went there always for my summer vacation with my mother because her parents were the rabbis in that city. It was very interesting for me because I grew up in a big city called Szeged, and the contrast was very interesting to me, even as a child. It was a small town, no indoor plumbing. That was fascinating-- outhouses. You had to get water from a well. And they had a-- in the summer, also, they had a school called a Cheder, where during the summer I would study. That's really how the vacation was, but I really enjoyed that small town life for-- for that period of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what age did you move to Szeged?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe my parents moved to Szeged in 1935, when I was about 2 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what part of Hungary is Szeged?

speaker: interviewee
text: Szeged is southern Hungary, on the-- just on the board of Yugoslavia, and it's considered the real Hungary. It's the second largest city in Hungary to this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your family life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents-- my father, was the orthodox rabbi of Szeged. Szeged had a large Jewish community, and they also had a Neolog community, which meant reform. But my father was the orthodox rabbi. And from the moment that I became aware of Jewish life, it was always tumultuous. In Europe, the rabbi's home was the Jewish hotel of the city. When a Jew would travel from one town to another, at night he would generally sleep by the rabbi. And especially in the years 1939 till '44, when we were deported, our home was just like a Grand Central. I never knew precisely which room I will sleep in, and there was constant activity, constant commotion, constant awareness of the troubles and travails and struggles of the Jewish people. There wasn't a day that we didn't see some new faces and everybody coming to my father and mother unloading their bitter hearts and telling them their stories. And-- my parents did not at all try to exclude the children from this. In fact, my father went out of his way to make sure that I heard everything; I saw everything, and he made me part of every story. And whenever he went even someplace, he would take me with him, which at that I didn't understand why, but now as an adult, I appreciate it. He exposed to the entire pain of the Jewish people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We had, I remember, food shortages. For example, my favorite breakfast was farina. I love farina to this day, and I never had enough. Every day my mother would give me farina, and I ask her, could I have some more? And was just so much, and I would lick the plate clean. I mention that because to this day, if you offer me a good steak or farina, I'll take farina. It's from those days. How many members were in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother, of course, and I have a younger sister who is a well-known rebbetzin today, Esther, and my younger brother, who is a rabbi, a practicing rabbi, and a great rabbinical scholar. He's unusually educated in rabbinics. He's what we call a [SPEAKING HEBREW]-- a very great scholar. And so we are three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to mention your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father, his name is Rabbi Abraham Jungreis [SPEAKING HEBREW] of blessed memory. He passed away 3 years ago, and he had a very active role [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the years of the Holocaust. He was very active and participated with our people during the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your education in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Our school was a few blocks away from where we lived. Usually, everything went by neighborhood. I lived, of course, in the Jewish neighborhood. And we had an unusual phenomenon in our city-- we had a day school, like you would have in America-- a day school. That was very unusual in Europe. It was run by the other Jewish community, what they call Neolog. In America today, they would call them modern orthodox, really, because the temple that is still in existence today, the Neolog temple, was one of the most beautiful temples in the world. The ladies sat upstairs on a balcony. The only difference is in an orthodox temple, the balcony itself would have a curtain on top of it. And the rabbi on the Shabbat spoke in Hungarian instead of Yiddish, and he wore a more tailored, long black coat, and his hat was not what they call a [SPEAKING HEBREW] hat, a wide [SPEAKING HEBREW] hat, but he wore a high hat. And that's why they call them Neolog or reform. In the United States, we call those people modern orthodox, or not even modern orthodox, just regular orthodox. That community ran a day school, and that's where I went to school. I learned how to read and write Hebrew, general Jewish studies, and general secular studies, how to read and write Hungarian, also, Hungarian history, and I recall to this day, and today somewhat cynically, how in that Jewish school, they emphasized loyalty to Hungary. That was very important. We swore allegiance to the flag every single day, the Hungarian flag. And when we mention the famous Hungarian hero, we would stand up and show respect. There was a tremendous emphasis, I would say an unusual emphasis, on loyalty to Hungary. Of course, this is how they paid us back. But I remember that. I also remember going to school was always traumatic. It was only three blocks from my house. I would be cursed at or beaten up every day. It was running a gauntlet, and they would start as soon as I would leave my house. [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN], which means dirty Jew, or you killed our God, which they said-- [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN], which means you killed our God.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, at the age of 5, I didn't understand what all this is about. In fact, I told my mother, who killed whose God? And how did I kill anybody's God? And then my mother explained to me the idea of anti-Semitism. But it was not proper at that time for a parent to accompany a child. There was no such thing as kidnapping in Europe, and would have looked sissy that my parents should have to walk me to the school. And it was very abusive every day. I felt I was really terrorized, and from that time on till I came to the United States, I think, I was in a constant state of fear. If someone asked me, how did you feel? This was the overriding feeling, always fear and afraid. You saw a policeman on the street, you would go onto the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they had special police. The regular policemen were somewhat civilized. Then they had the regular-- different police, what they call in English or French des gendarmes, or in Hungarian they cal them the [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN]. Those were like county police or state police. In the United States, you would call them rednecks, of the-- people of the lowest caliber. And they were brutes, very brutal, and you tried to avoid them. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this feeling of fear was just awful. I remember it. It was constantly with me, and you just can't escape it. There was just no escape from it. You felt constantly vulnerable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I even became a bed wetter because of that. It took a long time to get rid of that condition. It was so embarrassing. I couldn't go to people to sleep overnight. But it-- it was just-- that condition was with me till I came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was there a cultural life for the Jewish people in Szeged?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cultural life was mainly when you went to the schul. I remember my mother should be-- being the rebbetzin-- in Europe, the women, let's say, did not go to schul, except on certain holidays and certain occasions. And I remember when the women of the city came to our home. That was the night of [SPEAKING HEBREW] on the commemoration of the destruction of the temple, and my mother would read from a book to all the ladies who sat on the floor from [SPEAKING HEBREW]. It was called [SPEAKING HEBREW]. That was a rule that it was the duty, I believe, of the rebbetzin to do that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Also during Purim, she would use [SPEAKING HEBREW] for some women who can to the house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The cultural life was primarily [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] reserved just for the synagogue. We did not have, like, Jewish community centers. I believe that people were just inhibited. It was really not very safe to walk the streets, and people would be abused, many times, if they would walk the street. And the cultural life was limited to prayer time in the synagogue. And I might as well add the big cultural event was really for the men. The big social exchange, or the social events, were every morning, 5 o'clock, in the mikveh. Men went to the mikveh every morning-- that's the Jewish ritual bath-- before they went to pray. And in the mikveh, we would discuss-- for example, somebody would illegally listen in to the BBC. And because most people-- even in my home, we did not have a radio. It was a luxury to have a radio those days. And even if you had a radio, it was illegal to listen in to the BBC where you could hear the truth as to what's going on in the war. And everybody was looking forward to go to the mikveh every morning to listen-- to hear the report of these people who had the BBC, and in fact, we nicknamed it the mikveh radio. It was called the mikveh radio, and that was the only ray of hope. It was, like, usually encouraging news, because when you read the news in the Hungarian newspapers or the Hungarian radio, it was always depicting German victories or Hungarian victories, and it was slanted to their favor. The BBC would slant it in the opposite direction, and it gave us hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Yiddish mainly spoken between the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungary, they spoke Yiddish, but mainly Hungarian. Hungarian Jews [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were, I believe, very loyal to Hungary. It was a fairly decent place to live in. And most Jews spoke Hungarian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The rabbi in the schul, in the synagogue on Shabbat, would make his sermon in Yiddish, and the language of instruction in a cheder, let's say, in a shiva, would be Yiddish. It was unheard of to learn, let's say, the Talmud or Gemara in Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, when I came to America, I heard boys learning the Talmud Gemara in English, I was astounded. How can you learn Talmud in English? It was like a sacrilege.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yiddish was used primarily in connection with religious functions, but otherwise they would speak Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once the war broke out in 1939, a Shabbos and a Yom Tov, were you able to conduct it the same way? Was there a change?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not that much affected by the war as some of the other European countries. The-- the-- the big change came in the flow of refugees, and the anti-Semitism of the neighboring countries filtered in. For example, it wasn't the holidays as much as, let's say, travel. Travel-- exposing yourself to the Gentiles was always the problem, and Jews are a mobile group of people. We like to go to places. We like to hear things. We are curious. Especially rabbis always traveled and my father would go a lot to, let's say, Budapest or to visit his parents. And the only way to travel was by train. There were no buses, there were no cars. Some-- very few people had cars in those days. And traveling on the train, you expose yourself to all these horrible low-lives who would attack you, verbally abuse you, and physically abuse you. And their favorite game was-- my father, he would always come home bloody. They would rip out his beard on the train. So nobody was well to do that. The only solution was to travel second or first class, which costs two or three times as much as third class. But at the end, what happened was that Jews traveled less and they would travel in second class or first class, because there, even if they were anti-Semites, they camouflaged it more. Those people were somewhat more civilized, and they had a certain veneer, at least, and they knew not to be so blunt about it. And they had better behavior patterns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were Jewish boys taken into the Hungarian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the Jewish boys would be taken into the Hungarian army. And they would be the first ones to be sent to the front lines or to clear minefields. And most of them would not come back alive. As a matter of fact, I had an uncle who was a great scholar who died in the Hungarian uniform on the Ukrainian front line defending a country that's not his, defending a nation that's not his. But they sent him on the front line. I believe it was clearing a minefield, and that's how he died. We could not retrieve the body, but they notified the family that he died. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this was a very big headache, a very big problem. And later on-- excuse me-- later on they-- like around 1941-- they started to draft the Jewish boys in what they called [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN]-- war brigades. And that was not the army. That was labor brigades. And they would be sent to hard labor, and most of them would not come back alive from there, and our city where we lived was a center, an assembly center. Szeged-- we were on the Tisza River. And they would wait about once in two months to send a transport to a copper mine in Yugoslavia called Bor. These boats would take about 3,000 boys at the time. And my father visited these boys who were in these pup tents near the-- on-- on the riverside [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the docks. And we were involved specifically with two things in my family. One, when a boat would leave, my father would get up at about 3 o'clock in the morning, and he would take me along, which now only as an adult I understand-- I never understood why he did that-- and go to say goodbye to every single boy. In a big city like Szeged, they still had some respect for a rabbi. It was a university city and there was some respect. And they gave my father this privilege to say goodbye to every boy, and there was a little ceremony involved. At that time, the Hungarian quarter had a hole in the middle, and these boys would each take out this $0.25 piece called filler, 25 filler, and would put a chain around this coin, and with this coin my father gave them a blessing, and then they would put this coin and tie it on their necks as a [SPEAKING HEBREW], we call it in Jewish, a [SPEAKING HEBREW], or a charm for a good omen, for a blessing. And this goodbye procedure would take about seven, eight hours always, and standing. And I stood by, always watched it, and always watched the Hungarian officers and soldiers around smirking, ridiculing, laughing, smiling, screaming, 'it will not help. It will not help. It's a waste. It's not going to help them.' But my father had this inner strength. He did not pay attention to that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other thing we did was wherever possible, if someone was sick, then you missed the transport. So the idea was to get as many people as possible sick. And how do you get somebody sick? We had some very good Jewish doctors in our city and we asked them, what can we do to get people sick? We had about a dozen different gimmicks and tricks. Two I remember vividly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had these military hospitals where these sick boys would be, and we would smuggle into these hospitals these medications-- my mother in her bra and under her dress. Me and my father, we would take long socks and suspend it in our trousers, and fill it with these medications. And my sister, also, under her dress. Two of the medications were you took raw milk straight from a cow. And you injected that, and you would get an unusually high fever. And you became violently sick for about 48 hours, and you miss the transport. A second way of creating an ailment was you took soy beans, grind it into a powder, make it into a paste, and take this paste and smear it on your eyelids. And that would create an ailment resembling trachoma. And that was an infectious disease, and you missed the transport. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day, we come to the hospital loaded with this contraband, and in the hallway there, an officer stops us. We get scared. They usually-- the rabbi of the city, they don't stop you. My father, I remember, got very upset. He says, rabbi, don't worry. I want to tell you something.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you remember a few weeks ago, you were at the docks to say goodbye to the boys, and there was this officer who made fun of you, ridiculed you, and yelled that it will not help? As soon as we got to Bor, he went swimming, and he drowned. And he came to stop my father to tell him. What happened was he went with the transport and came back also.

speaker: interviewee
text: The parents of these boys would sleep by us. It was a very, very tragic experience every day to listen to the crying and emotional outpour, especially of the mothers. Once a very tragic event took place. Even within tragedies, there are many levels of tragedy. There was a boy, an 18-year-old, beautiful, handsome boy from a very well-known aristocratic family, from big [INAUDIBLE]. And he was taken to Szeged to be drafted into this work brigade, [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN]. He was absolutely brilliant, and he said he will know how to make himself sick. He is going to pretend he's crazy. He knew how to act crazy perfectly. They took him into a mental institution, and they gave him toys to play with. And he said, give me more toys. And he played, and every toy they gave him, he broke it, he said. He broke it purposely to inflict more of a burden on his tormentors. They even gave him a rocking horse once. He played with it and broke it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember this happened on a Shavuot holiday. This had to be in 1943. His mother was there by us for the holiday. And from our home, she went to visit because you could visit someone who is in a hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on Shavuot when she visited him, they said that he died. It seems that they gave him electric shock treatments, and he died. It was a horrible, most painful experience I remember to this day. I remember the-- I have even tears in my eyes now. I remember the agony of this mother. That was unusually painful. Sometimes it's hard to see the tree from the forest, but sometimes you focus on one case. That was most painful. I was also, even as a child-- I was terribly upset. That's how we celebrated Shavuot holiday in 1943. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In another case, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father did have some success. There was a boy from our city whom they also drafted, and then they accused him with all sorts of trumped-up charges in a far away location in northern Hungary. And we had a good lawyer in our city, and the parents asked this lawyer, now, what we have to consult? But they didn't let the lawyer go near a client. It wasn't even a client. There was no such things as a lawyer defending someone. But we have to tell this boy what to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only way to do this was that my father decided to travel to this town and tell the commander of the camp that he would like to have Friday night services. In the middle of the Friday night services, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the middle of singing the prayer [SPEAKING HEBREW], he digressed, and in Yiddish told this boy what to say when he's brought on trial, and his case was dismissed.
